title: Introduction to the Python GDS Client
Introduction
text: You've spent the last two modules using GDS in the Neo4j Browser—projecting graphs, running algorithms, and interpreting results. That foundation is solid.

title: Moving to Python
text: Now it's time to take those same skills into the environment where most real-world data science happens: Python.

title: Same algorithms, different syntax
text:
The Python GDS client isn't a different way of doing graph analytics. It's the same algorithms, the same concepts, the same workflows—just wrapped in a Pythonic interface that plays nicely with pandas, scikit-learn, and the rest of the Python ecosystem. 

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-1-intro-to-gds-python-s351-000-new]

Let's take a look at this diagram. On the left, you see the Neo4j Browser interface, and on the right, the Python GDS client. Notice how both sides illustrate the execution of the same graph algorithms and workflows, highlighting the differences in syntax and interface.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to set up a development environment for GDS work in Python, connect to Neo4j using the Python GDS client, execute Cypher queries and receive results as pandas DataFrames, and recognize when Python makes more sense than Browser, and vice versa.

title: Setting Up Your Environment
text: Before we write any code, let's get your development environment ready. Click the button below to open the workshop repository in a GitHub Codespace. This will clone the repository and set up a pre-configured Python environment automatically. The Codespace takes approximately 10 minutes to configure. While it's setting up, continue through the next few slides—we'll walk through the concepts before you need to run any code.

title: How the Python Client Works
text:
The GDS Python client acts as a bridge between your Python code and your Neo4j server. 

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-1-intro-to-gds-python-s354-002-new]

As you can see in this flowchart, it illustrates the connection between your Python environment, the GDS client, and the Neo4j server, showing the data flow and interaction.

title: GDS Python: Under the hood
text:
Under the hood, it translates your Python method calls into Cypher queries, sends them to the server, executes them against the GDS library, and returns results as pandas DataFrames. 

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-1-intro-to-gds-python-s355-003-new]

This diagram shows the process of converting Python method calls into Cypher queries, executing them on the Neo4j server, and returning results as pandas DataFrames. Notice the translation and execution steps.

title: Everything still applies
text: This means everything you learned about GDS in the Browser still applies. The algorithms haven't changed. The projections work the same way. You're just using a different interface to access them.

title: When to Use Python vs. Browser
text: Both tools have their place. The key is knowing which one fits your current task. Reach for Python when you're building repeatable data pipelines, automating workflows that run regularly, integrating graph analytics with other Python libraries, or working with results that need further processing. Stick with Browser when you're exploring data interactively, running quick, one-off queries, debugging projection or algorithm issues, or visually inspecting graph structure. For this module, we'll work primarily in Python—but you'll likely switch between both in practice.

title: Installing the Client
text: The official package is `graphdatascience`. In the Codespace you'll use, it's already installed. Otherwise, you can install it using pip with the command: `pip install graphdatascience`.

title: Connecting to Neo4j
text:
With the package installed, connecting is straightforward. You can use the following Python code to connect: 

```python
from graphdatascience import GraphDataScience

gds = GraphDataScience(
    "bolt://localhost:7687",
    auth=("neo4j", "password")
)

# Verify the connection works
print(gds.server_version())
```

By default, the client connects to the "neo4j" database. If your database has a different name, specify it explicitly like this: 

```python
gds = GraphDataScience(uri, auth=(user, password), database="my-db")
```

title: Running Cypher Queries
text:
Once connected, you can run any Cypher query using `gds.run_cypher()`. The results come back as a pandas DataFrame—ready for analysis, visualization, or further processing. Here's an example: 

```python
result = gds.run_cypher("""
    MATCH (m:Movie)
    RETURN m.title AS movie, m.year AS year
    ORDER BY m.year DESC
    LIMIT 10
""")

print(result.head())
```

This is useful for ad-hoc queries, but for GDS-specific operations like projections and algorithms, we'll use dedicated methods in the next lesson.

title: Closing Connections
text: When you're finished, close the connection to free up resources using `gds.close()`. Python will call this automatically when the `gds` object is garbage collected, but it's good practice to close connections explicitly—especially in notebooks where objects can persist longer than expected.

title: Our Dataset: The Cora Citation Network
text:
Throughout this module, we'll analyze a real academic citation network called Cora. What's in the dataset? It includes 2,708 papers spanning 7 research subjects, 10,556 citations, and 1,433-dimensional feature vectors. The seven research subjects are Neural Networks, Reinforcement Learning, Theory, Genetic Algorithms, Case-Based Reasoning, Probabilistic Methods, and Rule Learning. This is a classic dataset in machine learning research—small enough to iterate quickly, rich enough to demonstrate real patterns. 

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-1-intro-to-gds-python-s362-007-new]

Take a look at this structural diagram of the Cora citation network. It shows clusters of papers, directed citation edges, and feature vectors, with labels for the number of papers, citations, and feature dimensions.

title: What's Ahead
text:
With Python as our interface, we'll work through the complete GDS workflow: projecting graphs into memory, running algorithms like PageRank, Betweenness Centrality, Louvain, and FastRP, processing results as DataFrames, and cleaning up projections when we're done. Each algorithm will follow the same pattern: a deep-dive on the Movies dataset, which you know well, then hands-on practice with Cora. 

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-1-intro-to-gds-python-s363-008-new]

This flowchart outlines the GDS workflow in Python, from graph projection to algorithm execution and result processing, including steps for cleaning up projections.

title: Summary
text: The Python GDS client gives you programmatic access to everything you learned in the Browser: same algorithms, same projection logic, same workflows, with results returned as pandas DataFrames. Remember, version compatibility between client, driver, and GDS library matters. Your Codespace should be ready by now. In the next lesson, we'll connect to Neo4j and run our first GDS workflow entirely in Python.
